statement.markdown: Given an integer array nums, return all the triplets [nums[i], nums[j], nums[k]] such that i != j, i != k, and j != k, and nums[i] + nums[j] + nums[k] == 0. The solution set must not contain duplicate triplets.
statement.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer N, the number of elements in the array.
The second line contains N space-separated integers representing the array elements.
statement.outputFormat: Print each unique triplet on a new line, with elements separated by a space.

hints.text: To solve this problem, consider first sorting the input array to apply a two-pointer technique, which could simplify finding pairs that sum up to a target value. This approach might help in reducing the time complexity.

After sorting, iterate through the array and for each element, use two pointers starting from the next element and the last element of the array. The goal is to find a pair that sums up to the negation of the current element, thus making the total sum zero. Be cautious of duplicate triplets.

To avoid duplicate triplets, apply a skipping mechanism when iterating through the array and when moving the pointers. If the current element is the same as the previous one, skip it to avoid duplicates. Similarly, when the two pointers meet a pair that sums to the target, move them based on the sum and skip duplicates to ensure all unique triplets are found efficiently.

editorial.title: 3 Sum Problem Analysis
editorial.intuition: The brute force approach to solve this problem involves checking every possible triplet in the array, which results in a time complexity of O(n^3). However, this is inefficient and impractical for large inputs.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force solution is correct but slow due to its cubic time complexity, making it unsuitable for large inputs. It checks every possible combination of three elements in the array to find the triplets that sum up to zero.
editorial.betterSolution: A better approach is to sort the array first and then use a two-pointer technique to find the triplets. This reduces the time complexity to O(n^2).
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves sorting the array and then iterating over each element. For each element, it uses two pointers, one starting from the next element and one from the end of the array, to find a pair of elements that sum up to the negation of the current element. This approach ensures that each triplet is found exactly once and avoids duplicates by skipping over identical elements.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by showing that it covers all possible triplets and that the sorting and two-pointer technique ensure that each triplet is found exactly once. The time complexity of O(n^2) is due to the nested loops, and the space complexity of O(n) is due to the sorting and storage of the result.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the algorithm can be performed by taking a small sample input and manually iterating over the array to verify that the solution produces the correct output.
commonMistakes: Not sorting the array before applying the two-pointer technique
Not skipping over identical elements to avoid duplicates
Not correctly updating the pointers in the two-pointer technique
Not handling edge cases such as an empty input array or an array with less than three elements
Not considering the time and space complexity of the solution
